1:
The chapter opens on the winter of a great life, and the grammatical-historical commentators first fix the plain facts. Charles Ellicott notes that the opening "Now" is only the simple conjunction "and" that begins all the historical books, marking the unity of the sacred story without implying that the narrative runs on unbroken from Samuel; the history leaps at once to David's closing scene, leaving a comparative blank filled up only in 1 Chronicles 22. He reads "clothes" as bed-clothes, so that the meaning is that the king was now too feeble to rise. John Gill and Ellicott reckon him about seventy — thirty when he began to reign and forty years a king (2 Samuel 5:4) — while Adam Clarke makes him sixty-nine and presses that this was "no advanced age," for David was much older in constitution than in years, worn by fatigue and family affliction and by wasting maladies to which the Psalms so often allude. Keil and Delitzsch, opening the whole book, set the reign within the long arc of the monarchy the books of Kings will trace to its overthrow.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the bare notice into a meditation on mortality. David had just escaped the destroying angel of the foregoing chapter, but "our deliverances from or through diseases and dangers are but reprieves; if the candle be not blown out, it will burn out of itself." The valiant, active man of vehement flame now lies confined, his blood chilled, and no royal clothing can warm him — for if God deny his blessing, men clothe themselves and there is none warm (Haggai 1:6). Henry hears a double summons: "Let not the strong man glory in his strength," and let young people remember their Creator in the days of their youth (Ecclesiastes 12:1), doing with all their might what their hand finds to do before the night comes in which no man can work. He adds the sober note that David's premature decay was in part a chastisement for former sins, especially the matter of Uriah, and the fruit of youthful hardships he then made nothing of but was now the worse for.

Where the witnesses differ at all it is only in arithmetic — Clarke's sixty-nine against the seventy of Gill and Ellicott — and in emphasis. Clarke devotes his long preface to the making of the book itself, judging it the work of one compiler, most probably Ezra, who abridged older royal records with a priest's eye for the temple, the prophets, and the house of David; Keil agrees that the two books were originally one, divided by the Alexandrian translators. Beneath the scholarship the company is united in Henry's pastoral point: the mightiest king in Israel is here shown that strength is soon weakened by sickness, or at last by age, so that the wise will spend it well while it lasts.
2:
The king's decrepitude calls forth a strange prescription, and the witnesses divide over how to judge it. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as ancient medicine. Keil and Delitzsch explain that David's servants — his physicians, as John Gill notes, calling them servants just as Joseph's physicians are called his servants (Genesis 50:2) — sought a beautiful girl to be the aged king's nurse and to impart warmth, and they add the decisive historical caution: "the whole affair is to be judged according to the circumstances of the times, when there was nothing offensive in polygamy." Adam Clarke, the physician among the expositors, gives the medical rationale without embarrassment: David's "nearly exhausted frame would infallibly absorb from her young and healthy body an additional portion of animal heat, and consequently trim and revive the flame of animal life" — what he calls Friar Bacon's secret for the cure of old age.

The Puritan Matthew Henry alone registers moral unease. He thinks the physicians weak and unskilful, knowing no better remedy than outward applications, and faults them for prescribing nuptials to one who should rather have been preparing for his funeral. His prophets, Henry urges, should have been consulted as well as his physicians in an affair of this nature. Yet Henry will not condemn David by the standards of a later light: the thing "might be excused then, when even good men ignorantly allowed themselves to have many wives. We now have not so learned of Christ, but are taught that one man must have but one wife" (Matthew 19:5), and further that it is good for a man not to touch a woman (1 Corinthians 7:1).

The division, then, is one of register rather than fact. Keil weighs the matter as a historian and finds nothing scandalous by the custom of the age; Clarke weighs it as a doctor and finds it sound therapy; Henry weighs it as a moralist and finds it a lapse that only the ignorance of the times excuses. All three agree that Abishag was taken as David's wife and not merely his nurse — Henry infers it from the great crime it was later reckoned in Adonijah to desire her after his father's death (1 Kings 2:22), and Keil says the same. The verse that looks like a footnote about an old man's comfort is thus quietly planting the seed of the next chapter's fatal quarrel, and the witnesses are careful to mark it.
3:
The search for the king's nurse gives the grammatical-historical commentators their opening for geography and a famous conjecture. Charles Ellicott locates Shunem in the territory of Issachar (Joshua 19:18), in the plain of Jezreel near Mount Gilboa (1 Samuel 28:4); Eusebius, describing its position carefully, calls it "Sulem," a name confirmed by its ready identification with the modern village of Solam. On that thread Ellicott hangs the ingenious and, he thinks, not improbable conjecture that Abishag is the "fair Shulamite" of the Song of Solomon (Song of Solomon 6:13) — a guess that, if true, throws light on the darker occurrences of the following chapter (1 Kings 2:13–25).

The Reformed Gill attends to the single adjective the text presses: they sought not merely a damsel but a "fair" one, a beautiful girl who might be the more acceptable to the king; for otherwise, deformed or ill-favoured, she would not have been endured in his sight, nor would he have received things at her hand, much less suffered her to lie in his bosom. The beauty is not incidental ornament but part of the intended remedy, the youth and comeliness together meant to kindle warmth in the failing frame.

Ellicott is candid about why so slight a notice is preserved at all: "probably the whole notice of Abishag is only introduced on account of her subsequent connection with the fate of Adonijah." Here the witnesses agree with a rare unanimity of instinct — this is narrative economy. The inspired historian does not linger over the Shunammite for her own sake, and mentions her beauty and her province only because both will matter when, in the next chapter, Adonijah's request for her hand becomes the pretext of his ruin. The verse is a hinge disguised as a detail: it names the woman, fixes her homeland in the far northern plain, and quietly prepares the reader for a request that will cost a man his life. Even the modest search "throughout all the coasts of Israel" underscores how thoroughly the court exhausted the land to find the one damsel whose later story the writer means to tell, so that a line about an old king's comfort becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the overture to a tragedy.
4:
The verse ends the account of Abishag with a single guarded clause — "but the king knew her not" — and the witnesses read it not as a comment on David's virtue but as a deliberate preparation. Keil and Delitzsch are the most precise: the remark "is not introduced either to indicate the impotence of David or to show that she did not become David's concubine, but simply to explain how it was that it could possibly occur to Adonijah to ask for her as his wife" (1 Kings 2:17). The historian, in other words, forecloses in advance the objection the next chapter would otherwise raise: if Abishag were David's wife in the full sense, Adonijah's suit would be plain treason; the note that David "knew her not" leaves the question of her status open enough for the request to be made, and dark enough for Solomon to read treason in it.

The Reformed Gill lingers on the first half of the verse, where Keil hurries past it. The damsel "was very fair," and so very agreeable to the king, waiting on him and ministering to him, that he took things at her hand and she lay in his bosom to cherish him. Gill draws out the pathos of the scene the Hebrew states so plainly: the most beautiful woman in Israel is brought to a bedridden old man simply to keep him warm, her whole office reduced to attendance and comfort.

The Wesleyan Clarke keeps to his medical thread, quoting the maxim he ascribes to Bacon's "enigmatical cure": "Take all you can from the medicine, but give nothing to it; if you give any thing, it increases the disease and hastens death." He claims to have seen the principle abundantly verified, and treats David's continence as the very condition of the remedy's working. Between them the witnesses land on a quiet agreement: whatever the exact intent, the aged king remained chaste, and the clause is there less to praise his self-command than to set the stage. It is a masterstroke of the narrator's economy — a single denial that both honours the dying king and lights the fuse of the quarrel that will open the reign of his son.
5:
With Adonijah the chapter turns from a dying king to a grasping son, and the witnesses read him with one eye always on Absalom. The grammatical-historical commentators draw the parallel deliberately. Charles Ellicott renders his name "my Lord is Jehovah," makes him David's fourth son born in Hebron (2 Samuel 3:4), and infers from Solomon's later words (1 Kings 2:22) that he now claimed the throne as the eldest surviving son, Chileab having apparently died with Amnon and Absalom before him. The likeness to Absalom "in respect of personal beauty, favour with a too-indulgent father, ambition and trust in popularity, is evidently suggested by the narrative"; the fifty runners and the "chariots and horsemen" are exactly imitated from Absalom's example (2 Samuel 15:1), as Clarke also observes. Yet Ellicott finds Adonijah the lesser rebel, wanting the elder's craft and promptitude — his attempt "crude and ill-planned in conception." Keil and Delitzsch add the decisive theological frame: in Israel it was Jehovah, the God-King of his people, who reserved to himself the choice of the earthly king (Deuteronomy 17:15), a right he exercised for Saul, David, and Solomon alike, so that Adonijah's "I will be king" is a snatching at what God alone bestows.

The Reformed Gill keeps to the plain motive: his father being old and not likely to live long, and he the eldest and a comely person, Adonijah was "inspired with ambition to set up for king." The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the deeper offence. Adonijah "looked upon his father as superannuated and good for nothing," and it argues "a very base and wicked mind for children to insult over their parents because of the infirmities of their age." Above all, Henry hears in the usurpation a type of the world's revolt against the true King: knowing that by the designation of both God and David the crown was settled on Solomon (1 Chronicles 22:9; 1 Chronicles 23:1), Adonijah attempted by force to cut off the entail — "thus is the kingdom of Christ opposed, and there are those that say, We will not have him to reign over us" (Luke 19:14).

The voices do not divide so much as layer. To the historians Adonijah is a copyist of Absalom, imitating the trappings of rebellion without the genius; to Gill he is simple ambition seizing its moment; to Henry he is the perennial rebel against a settled and divine appointment. All agree that his self-exaltation runs against the grain of heaven's own choice, and that the very completeness of his mimicry of Absalom foreshadows the completeness of his fall.
6:
The narrator pauses to explain how such a son was made, and the witnesses converge, almost with one voice, on a failure of fatherly discipline. The Reformed Gill states it flatly: David had "always humoured him in everything, let him have his own way and will, and granted him what he desired, and never corrected him for his faults" — and he does not spare the king, judging this "not to the credit of David, being guilty of the same sin with Eli," whose indulgence of worthless sons brought down a house (1 Samuel 3:13). On that leniency, Gill says, Adonijah presumed, reckoning his father would not cross him now as he had never crossed him before.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as cause and effect: "such weak oversight on the part of his father encouraged him to make the present attempt." He notes too the detail the writer adds — Adonijah was "very beautiful," like Absalom (2 Samuel 14:25), and born after him — so that comeliness and birth-order together fed the young man's sense of a claim.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the fullest anatomy of the fault, and turns it into counsel for every household. David "had not displeased him at any time," not by crossing his humours, denying him anything, calling him to account, or reproving him — "he never said to him, Why hast thou done so? because he saw it was uneasy to him." Henry apportions the blame with care: it was the son's fault to be displeased at reproof and take it for an affront, and the father's fault that, seeing it displeased him, he forbore to reprove at all; "and now he justly smarted for indulging him." From this Henry draws the maxim that governs the scene: "Those who honour their sons more than God, as those do who keep them not under good discipline, thereby forfeit the honour they might expect from their sons." Indulged children learn to be proud and ambitious, "which is the ruin of a great many young people; the way to keep them humble is to keep them under." There is no debate here, only a united verdict from historian, Reformed divine, and Puritan pastor alike — the seed of Adonijah's rebellion was sown in his father's fondness, and David's own affliction in his son is a mirror held up to every parent who cannot bring himself to say, Why hast thou done so?
7:
Adonijah takes counsel with two great men of David's reign, and the witnesses labour to explain why such veterans would join so rash a cause. The grammatical-historical commentators offer both a narrow and a broad account. Keil and Delitzsch look to private grievance: Joab "had fallen out with David for a considerable time" (1 Kings 2:5–6) and hoped to secure his influence under a new king, while Abiathar's motive is owned to be obscure — "possibly jealousy of Zadok, and the fear that under Solomon he might be thrown still more into the shade." Charles Ellicott paints Joab in full as a "bold, hard, and unscrupulous man," imperious and often disobedient yet an absolutely loyal servant to whom David's throne largely owed its establishment, and Abiathar as the companion of all David's adversity (1 Samuel 22:20). But Ellicott reaches past personal motive to a larger reading that will recur through the chapter: perhaps the evident growth of despotic state in David's latter years had alienated the trusty friends of simpler days; and, decisively, "the attempt of Adonijah was not viewed as an actual rebellion." Solomon was young, David's designation of him might be dismissed as "the favouritism of dotage," and the assumption of the crown by the eldest son "might seem not only justifiable, but even right and expedient."

The Wesleyan Clarke reduces Joab's calculus to self-interest: he "well knew, if he made the new king, he would necessarily be continued in the command of the army, and so govern him." The Reformed Gill supplies the plainer politics — the general commanded the soldiery and the high priest the affections of the people, and it was his office to anoint a king; Adonijah applied to them the more readily "knowing them to be, on some accounts, discontented persons."

The real difference among the witnesses is one of depth. Clarke and Keil trace the defection to ambition and pique; Gill to discontent and usefulness; Ellicott to a whole climate of feeling in which Adonijah's move looked less like treason than like the natural succession of the eldest. That last suggestion does much to explain the chapter's later course — why the conspiracy collapses so quietly, and why, of all the guests, Adonijah alone is left in dread. The witnesses agree at least on the tragedy of it: two men who had stood by David through every hardship now lend their weight to the one son whose cause God had already set aside.
8:
Over against Adonijah's party the writer sets the roll of those who would not join him, and the grammatical-historical commentators take the verse as a muster of David's true men. Charles Ellicott identifies each in turn: Zadok, son of Ahitub, representative of the family of Eleazar, Aaron's elder son, as Abiathar was of the line of Ithamar (1 Chronicles 24:3); Benaiah, of Levitical stock, captain of the bodyguard, ranking between "the three mighty men" and "the thirty" (2 Samuel 23:23); and Nathan the prophet, who through this whole chapter "appears rather as a chief officer and counsellor of David, than in the loftier aspect of the prophetic character," being also the royal chronicler of the reign (1 Chronicles 29:29). Of the obscurer names, Shimei and Rei, he can only report Ewald's conjecture that they were brothers of David, while the Gibborim, "the mighty men," he takes for a technical name — the standing nucleus of Israel's armies, successors of the six hundred of David's wandering (2 Samuel 23:8). Keil and Delitzsch simply oppose the two lists: against Joab and Abiathar stand all the rest of the great officers of state.

The Reformed Gill catches the note that gives the verse its edge — Zadok, he observes, "bid fair to be the high priest on Solomon's coming to the throne, as he was." For the division here is not merely political but priestly. Adonijah has drawn Abiathar, of Eli's fallen house; loyalty gathers around Zadok of the elder line. As Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note of that older reversal, the high priesthood, once withdrawn from Eleazar's line and lodged with the house of Ithamar to which Eli belonged, was now, on the forfeiture of his descendants, "to be taken from them and restored to the elder branch" — the very sifting this chapter sets in motion, and which the word to Eli's house had foretold (1 Samuel 2:35). Adam Clarke adds only the tradition that Nathan may have been Solomon's tutor, a bond that would explain his zeal.

The witnesses do not dispute; they read a single tableau. On one side a general with a grudge and a priest of a doomed line; on the other the future high priest, the captain of the guard, the prophet, and the veteran corps who had never failed David. The muster of names is the writer's quiet assurance that the weight of Israel's tried fidelity lay, from the first, on Solomon's side.
9:
Adonijah crowns his preparations with a great sacrificial banquet, and the witnesses read the scene as a coronation staged under the colour of religion. The grammatical-historical commentators map the ground. Keil and Delitzsch mark that he "commenced his usurpation, like Absalom" (2 Samuel 15:2), "with a solemn sacrificial meal, at which he was proclaimed king, at the stone of Zocheleth by the side of the fountain of Rogel," below the junction of the valley of Hinnom with the valley of Jehoshaphat. Charles Ellicott confesses the name Zoheleth uncertain — from a root meaning to "crawl" or "steal on," rendered variously "the stone of serpents," "the rolling stone," or "the stone of the conduit" — and identifies En-rogel as "the spring of the fuller" (Joshua 15:7), the only natural spring near Jerusalem, its proximity useful for the purposes of sacrifice.

The Wesleyan Clarke fastens on that word "sacrifice." Having Abiathar the priest with him, Adonijah offered the animals sacrificially and then feasted on the flesh of the victims; "he had not only a splendid feast, but a great sacrifice, and he gave by this a popular color to his pretensions, by affecting to receive his authority from God." The Reformed Gill keeps to the politics of the table — a large and elegant entertainment to bind a numerous party, some of them of the first rank.

Here the Puritan Matthew Henry adds the sharpest moral. He suspects, like Clarke, that Adonijah "began his usurpation with a show of devotion, as Absalom under the colour of a vow" (2 Samuel 15:7), the more plausibly for having the high priest at his side; and he laments the abuse with a proverb: "In nomine Domini incipit omne malum — In the name of the Lord begins all evil," when religious exercises are made to patronise irreligious practices. The witnesses converge on a single judgement of the feast: it is a counterfeit consecration. Where Solomon will shortly be anointed at Gihon by prophet and priest at David's own command, Adonijah at En-rogel manufactures the trappings of divine sanction — the sacrifice, the acclamation, the great men at meat — to cloak a throne that God has not given, and the very borrowing of Absalom's script is a token that his end will be Absalom's too.
10:
The list of the uninvited closes the account of the conspiracy, and the witnesses read the guest-list itself as the plot's confession. The Reformed Gill draws out each pointed omission. Nathan the prophet was not called, for Adonijah "might know had prophesied of Solomon's succession in the throne, and therefore it could not be thought he would be drawn over"; nor Benaiah and the mighty men, David's bodyguard; "and still less Solomon, his competitor and rival." The banquet that gathered "all the king's sons" was careful to leave out precisely those who knew God's mind, held the swords of the guard, or stood next in the true succession.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the exclusion of Solomon as a study in the usurper's contempt: Solomon "was not invited, either because he despised him or because he despaired of him." Either way the omission is not oversight but design, and it becomes in the sequel the very proof of treason that Bathsheba and Nathan will press before the king — "but Solomon thy servant hath he not called" (1 Kings 1:19; 1 Kings 1:26). A conspiracy that must exclude the lawful heir, the prophet, and the guard has already named its own guilt.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch let the verse do its narrative work, for the same clause that ends the feast opens the counter-move: "Adonijah's attempt was frustrated by the vigilance of the prophet Nathan," who now turns to Bathsheba. And here the witnesses find the quiet hand of providence. The men Adonijah dared not invite were exactly the men able to undo him — the prophet who carried heaven's designation, the captain who commanded the troops, the son whom God had chosen. By shutting them out to secure his secret, the usurper left intact the very coalition that would within hours proclaim Solomon at Gihon. The verse that seems only to finish a roll of names is, in the witnesses' reading, the turning of the tide: the plot is complete, and in its completeness it has excluded everything that could have made it succeed, so that the omission of Solomon becomes the opening through which Solomon's kingdom comes.
11:
The prophet's entrance changes the chapter's whole current, and the witnesses read his intervention as wisdom armed with a divine commission. The grammatical-historical commentators explain why Nathan of all men should act. Charles Ellicott finds the initiative "especially natural, since he had been the medium both of the prophecy to David of the son who should build the Lord's house" (2 Samuel 7:12–15) "and also of the blessing on Solomon, embodied in the name Jedidiah" (2 Samuel 12:25) — and adds the shrewd note that "perhaps for this very reason the conspirators had altogether held aloof from him." Keil and Delitzsch summarise the counsel: Nathan told Bathsheba that Adonijah "had become as good as king," and advised her to save her own life and her son's by reminding David of his sworn promise.

The Puritan Matthew Henry paints the three who slept while the plot ran on. David "knew not what was doing"; Bathsheba "lived retired, and knew nothing of it either, till Nathan informed her"; and Solomon, who likely knew, "was as a deaf man that heard not," neither stirring nor agitating, but leaving it to God and his friends — for while men in pursuit of the world "in vain rise early and sit up late, God giveth his beloved sleep" (Psalms 127:2). Nathan, by contrast, could not sit still, "because he knew God's mind, and David's and Israel's interest"; it was by him that God had named Solomon Jedidiah, and so he applied to Bathsheba as the one with the deepest concern and the freest access.

Henry draws the counsel out to its evangelical point. Nathan warned that not only Solomon's crown but their very lives were at stake, and Henry reminds us that "the law of self-preservation, and the sixth commandment, obliges us to use all possible endeavours to secure our own life and the life of others." Such, he says, "is the counsel that Christ's ministers give us in his name, to give all diligence, not only that no man take our crown" (Revelation 3:11) "but that we save our lives, even the lives of our souls." Adam Clarke adds only that Adonijah was now "considered as being legally appointed to the regal office." The witnesses hold together a double lesson: Solomon rests in God while Nathan bestirs himself for God — faith and diligence, the quiet trust of the heir and the wakeful zeal of the prophet, working the one deliverance.
12:
Nathan turns from alarm to counsel, and the witnesses read the verse as prudence roused by mortal danger. The grammatical-historical commentators lay bare the political logic. Keil and Delitzsch take the plain sense — the counsel is given "that thou mayest save thine own life, and the life of thy son Solomon" — and Charles Ellicott supplies the reasoning behind the fear: "the usurpation of Adonijah would, as a matter of course, be sealed by the blood of his rival Solomon" (2 Chronicles 21:4). Bathsheba herself need hardly have been sacrificed, he allows, "but her position of favour with David would excite jealousy," and Solomon, still young, "might well be thought only an instrument in her hands."

The Reformed Gill attends to Bathsheba's state of mind. A woman "surprised and confounded at this relation," she might well "be at a loss what to do"; and so Nathan, "being a wise man, and a faithful friend," offers the best advice he can and asks her attention to it. The scene is one of a seasoned counsellor steadying a frightened mother.

The Wesleyan Clarke rises from the particular case to a moralist's lament and a political digression. Nathan, he says, "took for granted that Adonijah would put both Bath-sheba and Solomon to death as state criminals," and the thought wrings from him an outcry: "O cursed lust of rule! a father will destroy his son, a son depose his father, and a brother murder a brother, in order to obtain a crown!" From the unsettled state of the succession — where "no man knew who was to succeed to the crown" — Clarke turns aside to argue his own conviction that "hereditary monarchy has a high balance of evidence in its favor beyond that which is elective, and is every way more safe for the state and more secure for the subject." It is a characteristic Clarke excursion, reaching from the text into the science of government, where the others keep to the danger at hand. The witnesses do not contend; they agree that the peril was real and the counsel wise, and that beneath the palace intrigue lies the naked truth Clarke names — the lust of rule that will spill even a brother's blood to seize a throne.
13:
Nathan's instruction is precise: go to David, recall his oath, and ask why Adonijah reigns. The witnesses fasten on the oath itself, which the narrative has not recorded before. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is candid: "of this oath we have no mention elsewhere. It may have belonged to the time of Solomon's birth" (2 Samuel 12:24–25). He points also to the earlier designation of Solomon (1 Chronicles 22:6–13), and draws from it the governing principle of the whole succession: "according to Oriental custom, such designation, without strict regard to priority of birth, lay in the prerogative of the reigning king." The crown is David's to bequeath by God's leave, not Adonijah's to claim by birth.

The Wesleyan Clarke agrees that the oath was real though private — "known only to Bath-sheba and Nathan; it is nowhere else mentioned" — and he reads Nathan's choice of messenger shrewdly: "he knew that this woman had a sovereign influence over the king." But Clarke also hears in the errand a deeper poignancy. "If Bath-sheba was a source of pleasure to David, must she not also have been a source of pain to him? For could he ever forget the guilty manner in which he acquired her?" The very woman sent to secure the succession is the living reminder of the sin that first brought her to David's house, so that mercy and memory meet in the one petitioner.

The Reformed Gill keeps the counsel practical: she is to go into the chamber where the king lay "at once, without any ceremony," and put the question plainly. The witnesses agree on the substance even where the record is thin: an oath was sworn, most likely at Solomon's birth, and the right to name a successor belonged to the king. Where they add colour is in the human texture — Ellicott in the constitutional custom, Clarke in the sovereign influence and the shadow of an old guilt, Gill in the urgency of a message that must not wait on ceremony. The unrecorded oath becomes the hinge of the appeal: David's own word, bound by the name of the LORD, is the surest lever to move a conscientious king.
14:
Nathan completes his plan with a stroke of court craft: he will enter behind Bathsheba and confirm her words. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on what the maneuver reveals about David's court. Charles Ellicott reads in it "a growth of royal state and Oriental reverence for the king's person since the defeat of Absalom," a majesty that will swell still further under Solomon. Bathsheba's very entrance "into the bedchamber seems to be looked upon as an intrusion, to be ventured upon only in the humble attitude of a suppliant," and "Nathan does not presume to approach the king with remonstrance, till the maternal anxiety of Bath-sheba has paved the way." He sets beside it the still more unapproachable royalty of Persia, where even a queen dared not come unbidden (Esther 4:10–16). Keil and Delitzsch gloss the key verb: Nathan would "make her word full" — not supply what is wanting but "confirm it by similar assertion," like the Greek to fulfil.

The Reformed Gill catches the timing — Nathan would come in "before, or by the time she could deliver the above words," while the king was being awakened to the interest of Bathsheba and her son. And behind the scene stands the principle the Puritan Matthew Henry names from the block: Nathan "would come in and second her," so that "out of the mouth of two witnesses, two such witnesses, the word would be established," and David, hearing the same account twice, would give not a dilatory answer but positive orders at once (compare Deuteronomy 19:15).

The witnesses read no conflict here, only converging admiration for the prophet's wisdom. To Ellicott the scene is a window on the hardening ceremonial of an aging court, where access is guarded and approach must be earned; to Keil it is a lesson in the Hebrew idiom of confirmation; to Henry and Gill it is a model of how a good cause is best carried — not by one voice easily deferred, but by two agreeing witnesses whose testimony compels decision. Nathan's care to second Bathsheba is neither timidity nor flattery but the shrewd humility that reads the room, waits its moment, and so turns a sick king's hesitation into resolve. The stagecraft is entirely in the service of the truth, and the truth is that Solomon, not Adonijah, is the LORD's appointed heir.
15:
Bathsheba obeys, and the narrator lets us see the room she enters. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the scene: she "went to the king into the inner chamber, since the very aged king, who was waited upon by Abishag, could not leave his room." Keil also defends the text against those who would smooth it, noting that the repeated "And now, behold, Adonijah has become king; and now, my lord king, thou knowest it not" is no scribal error but "may be explained from the energy with which Bathsheba speaks" — the doubled phrase carrying the urgency of a frightened mother pleading for her son.

The Reformed Gill simply follows her in: she "took Nathan's advice, and directly went to the king's apartment," where he lay bedridden. The detail matters, for it shows the counsel of the previous verses already in motion — no delay, no ceremony, the mother going straight to the one man who could save her.

It is the Puritan Matthew Henry who draws the character-note from the scene. He observes that "when she visited the king Abishag was ministering to him," and "Bathsheba took no displeasure either at him or her for it" — no jealousy of the young Shunammite at the dying king's side, only the single-minded purpose of her errand. And he marks her reverence: "she bowed and did obeisance to the king, in token of her respect to him both as her prince and as her husband; such a genuine daughter was she of Sarah, who obeyed Abraham, calling him lord" (1 Peter 3:6). Henry turns it to a rule: "those that would find favour with superiors must show them reverence, and be dutiful to those whom they expect to be kind to them." The witnesses agree in reading the moment as a small portrait of humility and self-command — a queen who could have stood on her rights bowing low before a bedridden husband, keeping her eyes not on the beautiful nurse but on the promise she had come to claim, and pressing her plea with an energy the very grammar of the passage preserves.
16:
The scene narrows to a gesture and a question. Bathsheba "bowed, and did obeisance to the king," and David answers, "What wouldest thou?" The Reformed Gill reads the double reverence as itself a kind of speech: she bowed "not only as being her husband, but her sovereign," and the very depth of the homage "might intimate that she had something to say to him, and more than to inquire of his health." The bow is a petition before a word is spoken; the king, seeing it, opens the way with his question.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as the threshold of the whole address, setting out the speech that follows through to the entrance of Nathan: the reminder of the oath, the news that Adonijah reigns, the tell-tale guest-list, the eyes of all Israel, and the peril to mother and son. The obeisance of this verse is the formal opening of a carefully ordered plea.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the address as a whole, calls it "very discreet," and the discretion begins here, in the posture. Bathsheba does not burst in with accusation; she bows, waits to be asked, and only then speaks. It is the pattern the previous verse commended — reverence that wins a hearing — now enacted at the decisive moment. The witnesses find in the exchange a quiet lesson in how petitions are best made to the great: not by presuming on relationship, though she was his wife, nor on standing, though she was a queen, but by rendering honour first and speaking second. David's "What wouldest thou?" is the answer such reverence draws out — the king's readiness to hear a suit humbly urged. The moment is slight in words but weighty in bearing, for on the success of this interview hangs the succession itself, and Bathsheba's whole hope rests on moving a conscientious old man to remember and to act. She begins, therefore, not with Adonijah but with a bow, and lets her respect prepare the ground for the oath she is about to plead. Gill and Henry alike see in the gesture the whole temper of a wise suppliant: she trusts her cause to the king's justice and to his sworn word, and prepares him to grant it by first granting him the honour due a prince and a husband.
17:
Bathsheba opens her plea where Nathan told her to open it — on David's own oath. The Reformed Gill weighs the force of her appeal: she reminds the king that he "swarest by the Lord thy God unto thine handmaid," and Gill marks it "a very solemn oath, and binding," pressed upon a man she knew to be "so conscientious" that he "would religiously observe it." Her strategy is not to argue Solomon's merits but to bind the king by his own sworn word, sealed in the name of the LORD his God.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, reading the speech as a whole, sees the same wisdom. She "reminded him of his promise made to her and confirmed with a solemn oath, that Solomon should succeed him," because "she knew how fast this would hold such a conscientious man as David." The oath is the surest of levers: conscience, as well as a sense of honour, would "put life into him upon such an occasion as this," rousing the failing king to defend a settlement he had bound himself before God to keep.

The witnesses, few here but agreed, draw out the weight of an oath taken in the divine name. To swear "by the Lord thy God" was to summon God himself as witness and surety, so that to break the word would be to profane the name invoked. Bathsheba's appeal thus reaches past David's affection for her or for Solomon to something deeper — the fear of God in a man who, for all his failings, held an oath sacred. She calls herself "thine handmaid," keeping the posture of the suppliant even as she presses the strongest claim she has; and she quotes the promise back to him almost word for word — "Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne" — so that David hears his own vow returned to him. It is the quiet genius of her opening that she asks nothing new, only that the king be true to himself and to the God by whom he swore. The rest of her speech will supply the facts; this first stroke supplies the obligation, and it is on the obligation that everything turns.
18:
Having laid down the oath, Bathsheba states the crisis: "And now, behold, Adonijah reigneth." The Reformed Gill notes the delicacy with which she frames it. Adonijah "has set up himself as king, and is by some saluted as such"; yet, "lest it should be thought by David that she suggested by this that he was guilty of the breach of his oath, or on any account to be blamed," she is careful to add that the king knows nothing of it. She accuses the son without impeaching the father.

The Wesleyan Clarke had already caught the gravity of the report from Nathan's first announcement: Adonijah "was now considered as being legally appointed to the regal office, and no doubt was about to begin to perform its functions." This is no distant threat but an accomplished fact — a rival already reigning, already receiving the acclamations of a court.

The Puritan Matthew Henry catches the tact of Bathsheba's whole manner: "the fault is not thine, for thou knewest it not; but now that thou knowest it thou wilt, in pursuance of thy promise, take care to suppress this usurpation." Her art is to leave David his honour while robbing him of his excuse. She does not say, You have broken your word; she says, in effect, A thing has been done behind your back that only you can undo. The witnesses read the verse as a small masterpiece of persuasion. Bathsheba knows that to reproach a dying king would harden him; to inform him, while clearing him, is to enlist his conscience on her side. The clause "thou knowest it not" does two things at once — it exposes the secrecy of the plot, which is itself a token of its guilt, and it hands David the one honourable course still open to him. The usurpation is real and advancing; the king is innocent but no longer ignorant; and therefore, by the logic Bathsheba has so carefully built, he is now bound both by his oath and by his knowledge to act. The tenderness of her wording is not weakness but calculation in the best sense — the calculation of a wise petitioner who would win the king rather than wound him.
19:
Bathsheba turns from the bare fact of the usurpation to its damning detail — the guest-list — and the witnesses read the omission of Solomon as her sharpest thrust. The Reformed Gill first marks the affront to David himself: Adonijah "hath slain oxen, and fat cattle, and sheep in abundance," making "a grand entertainment," and is "feasting and rejoicing" while his aged father lies "on his dying bed" — carousing "in the view of his death, and wishing for it." The banquet is an insult to a dying king.

But the deeper point, as the Puritan Matthew Henry draws it out, lies in who was left off the roll. Bathsheba "told him who were Adonijah's guests, and who were in his interest, and added, but Solomon thy servant hath he not called, which plainly shows he looks upon him as his rival, and aims to undermine him." Henry presses the conclusion the exclusion forces: "it is not an oversight, but a contempt of the act of settlement, that Solomon is neglected." The very care with which Adonijah gathered the king's sons, and Abiathar, and Joab, while pointedly passing over Solomon, betrays that he knew exactly whose claim he was supplanting.

The witnesses thus make the guest-list an argument. It had first appeared, in 1 Kings 1:10, as the writer's own quiet notice that Nathan, Benaiah, and Solomon were not called; now Bathsheba turns that same fact into evidence before the king. To feast the great and omit the heir is not neutral; it is a public declaration that Solomon has no place in the new order. Gill and Henry agree that this is the stroke calculated to move David most, for it shows the usurpation aimed squarely at the son the king had sworn to enthrone. The feast at En-rogel, meant to bind a party, becomes in Bathsheba's mouth the proof of Adonijah's design — and the neglected name of "Solomon thy servant," set against the crowded table of the conspirators, is the whole case for immediate action, urged not as accusation but as plain, undeniable report. So the neglected name becomes the crux of the appeal, and the crowded table at En-rogel its dark foil: the one man Adonijah dared not seat is the one man David had sworn to enthrone, and Bathsheba lets that silence speak.
20:
Bathsheba now lifts the matter from private grievance to public expectation: "the eyes of all Israel are upon thee, that thou shouldest tell them who shall sit on the throne." The grammatical-historical Clarke states the constitutional fact the verse assumes: "this was a monarchy neither hereditary nor elective; the king simply named his successor," a usage that "obtained less or more, anciently, in most countries." The nation waits not for a birthright to assert itself nor for a vote to be taken, but for David to speak.

The Reformed Gill grounds the appeal in the people's own longing — the throne's future "will appear from the expectations of the people," who look to the king to settle it. Bathsheba speaks not only for herself and Solomon but for a watching nation whose peace hangs on David's word.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the point higher still. All Israel looks to David "not only as a king" — for it is no prince's prerogative "to bequeath his subjects by will, as if they were his goods and chattels" — "but as a prophet." All knew that "the Spirit of the Lord spoke by him," and so in a matter of such moment they waited for "a divine designation," knowing that "a divine sentence is in the lips of the king" (Proverbs 16:10). Henry turns it to a charge upon all who hold any influence: "whatever power, interest or influence, men have, they ought to improve it to the utmost for the preserving and advancing of the kingdom of the Messiah, of which Solomon's kingdom was a type." The witnesses converge from their several angles on one truth: the succession rests on the king's designation, and David's designation is no mere political act but a prophetic one, an oracle the whole nation awaits. Bathsheba's flattery, if it be flattery, is also theology — she reminds David that his word here will carry the authority of God's own choice, and that all Israel, from the servants at En-rogel to the mother at his bedside, hangs upon his sentence. Thus Bathsheba's closing appeal is at once political and prophetic: she asks the king to exercise a prerogative all Israel acknowledges, and to exercise it as the mouthpiece of the God whose Spirit had so often spoken by him, settling in one word the peace of a watching nation.
21:
Bathsheba closes her plea with the peril that will follow David's death: "otherwise it shall come to pass, when my lord the king shall sleep with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon shall be counted offenders." The grammatical-historical Ellicott lingers on the phrase that will echo through these books. Here "sleep with his fathers," so constantly used afterward of the death of the kings, "occurs in these books for the first time," and though he will not read into it the full New Testament "sleep in Jesus," he thinks it "not unreasonable to recognise in it, at least, a rudimentary belief in death as rest and not extinction," with corresponding expressions in the patriarchs (Genesis 15:15; Deuteronomy 31:16). The addition "with his fathers," he notes, probably points to "the tombs of the kings," for it is withheld from those later kings not buried there (2 Chronicles 21:20; 2 Chronicles 24:25).

The Reformed Gill hears the same hope in the word: to sleep with the fathers is to "die, and be buried in the sepulchre of his ancestors, where he shall lie till he awakes in the morning of the resurrection." Death is a sleep from which there is a waking.

The Wesleyan Clarke translates the danger into blunt terms: "shall be counted offenders" means that "when Adonijah and his party shall find that I and my son have had this promise from thee by oath, he will slay us both." The witnesses read the verse as the culmination of Bathsheba's whole argument. She began with the oath and ends with the grave, and the two are joined: the very mortality that opened the chapter — the king who could get no heat — is now the ground of her urgency, for once David sleeps with his fathers there will be no one to shield the heir. The gentle phrase for dying carries a sharp edge; it reminds the king that his protection is a wasting asset, and that the settlement he swore must be made secure now, in his life, or it will be sealed in the blood of his wife and son. Mortality, rest, and peril meet in a single sentence, and they land David where Bathsheba means to land him — on the necessity of acting today.
22:
The narrative marks the exact moment of Nathan's arrival — "while she yet talked with the king" — and the witnesses read the timing as the very stroke Nathan had planned. The Reformed Gill fixes it precisely: it was "just as she was concluding her speech to him" that the prophet came in, so that the two testimonies overlap and the second confirms the first before the king can frame a hesitant reply.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the design behind the coincidence. Nathan came "according to his promise, seasonably... and seconded her, while she was speaking, before the king had given his answer, lest, if he had heard Bathsheba's representation only, his answer should be dilatory and only that he would consider of it." The aim was decision, not delay; and Henry adds that the arrangement worked upon the king's own habits of mind, for David might well look upon the prophet's timely entrance "as a special providence," being one who took notice of such evidences.

The verse, brief as it is, marks a hinge in the interview: Bathsheba has laid the whole case, and now a second and weightier witness steps forward to seal it. Henry observes that David might have answered a mother's plea with a father's caution, weighing it, sleeping on it, letting the crisis run; but the sudden appearance of Nathan — the prophet who had named Solomon Jedidiah and carried God's word to the house of David — forecloses that ease. The two witnesses that the previous verses had arranged now stand together in fact, and the principle Henry named comes into force: out of the mouth of two witnesses the word is established, and a matter twice attested demands to be settled. The narrator's care to note that Nathan entered while Bathsheba still spoke is thus no idle detail but the pivot of the scene, the moment when a private petition becomes a public confirmation and a sick king's indecision gives way, under the double witness of queen and prophet, to the resolve that the next verses will record. Providence and prudence meet in the doorway, and the succession turns upon a well-timed entrance.
23:
Nathan is announced and comes before the king with deep reverence, and the witnesses attend to the prophet's bearing. The Reformed Gill supplies the setting — "some that attended at the door, or were in the chamber" told the king, "Behold Nathan the prophet" — and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the homage: "when he was come in before the king, he bowed himself before the king with his face to the ground," the same lowly obeisance Bathsheba had made.

The Puritan Matthew Henry finds in that bow a lesson in the honour due to office. Nathan "pays the king the same respect now that he finds him sick in bed as he would have done if he had found him in his throne: He bowed himself with his face to the ground." A dying king is a king still, and the prophet who will presently speak with prophetic boldness first renders the reverence of a subject. Yet Henry marks the difference of manner too: Nathan "deals a little more plainly with the king than Bathsheba had done," for "his character would support him, and the present languor of the king's spirits made it necessary that they should be roused."

The witnesses read the moment as a study in the two capacities Nathan holds together. Keil elsewhere observes how, through this chapter, Nathan appears less in the lofty aspect of the prophet than as a counsellor of state; and here the blend is on display. He bows to the ground like any courtier, honouring the king's person, and then addresses him with a directness no courtier would dare, honouring the king's need. The reverence is not flattery and the plainness is not insolence; each serves the same end, which is to rouse a failing king to his duty. Henry's point is that true faithfulness knows how to combine the two — to give honour where honour is due and yet to speak the searching word the moment requires. Nathan's face to the ground and Nathan's blunt question belong together, the humility of the subject and the boldness of the prophet meeting in one man, so that David is at once respected and awakened, and the interview moves toward its decisive close.
24:
Nathan opens not with accusation but with a question that pretends ignorance: "My lord, O king, hast thou said, Adonijah shall reign after me?" The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the artfulness of it. The question "here and in 1 Kings 1:27 is, of course, merely intended to draw out denial; but it is singularly true to nature that it does so by the assumption, natural in court language, that nothing of such a kind could be even conceived as done without the king's will." Nathan frames the whole crisis as a thing impossible unless David himself had willed it — and so compels the king to disown it.

The Reformed Gill notes the diplomatic pretence: Nathan "addresses him with great veneration and respect due to his office, so as if he knew nothing of Bathsheba's application to him," beginning his story "as if the king had never heard anything relative to it." The two witnesses are careful not to appear rehearsed; Nathan tells the tale afresh, as though bringing news.

Ellicott draws a further contrast that lights up Nathan's whole role in the chapter. There is "something striking in the contrast of the deference of Nathan as a counsellor on state business with the bold superiority of his tone in the discharge of his true prophetic office" — the man who here bows and defers is the same who once stood before David and said, "Thou art the man" (2 Samuel 12:7). The witnesses agree that the question is a piece of holy shrewdness. By asking whether David had ordered Adonijah's reign, Nathan does three things at once: he clears himself of any charge of meddling, he flatters the king's authority by treating his will as the only conceivable source of such an act, and he forces David to a clear disavowal that will commit him to Solomon. The feigned ignorance is not deceit but tact in the service of truth, the courtier's manner enlisted to secure the prophet's cause. And beneath the deference lies the same authority that had once rebuked the king to his face — an authority now bent, gently and cannily, to rouse him to defend the son God had chosen.
25:
Nathan now lays out the evidence, and the witnesses note both the facts and the ominous cry that crowned the feast. The Reformed Gill traces the geography of the report: Adonijah "is gone down this day" — down "from Jerusalem, which lay high, to the stone of Zoheleth, in Enrogel, which lay in the valley." The very verb marks the descent from the royal city to the spring below, where the rival court had gathered.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes on the acclamation Nathan reports: "they eat and drink before him, and say, God save king Adonijah." Literally, he explains, the shout is "May the king live" — the same formula found when Israel first hailed Saul (1 Samuel 10:24) and used again of Absalom (2 Samuel 16:16) — the very cry that will presently be raised for Solomon, and that Bathsheba had echoed in her own "Let my lord king David live for ever" (1 Kings 1:31). The words that ought to belong to the LORD's anointed are here shouted over a usurper.

The witnesses read Nathan's recital as a careful marshalling of the case. He names the sacrifice, the abundance of slain cattle, the guests — "all the king's sons, and the captains of the host, and Abiathar the priest" — and the treasonous acclamation, and he presses the same tell-tale point Bathsheba had pressed: those pointedly not invited. The whole account is designed to leave David in no doubt that a rival reign has already been proclaimed with the full trappings of royalty, feast and priest and shout together. Gill's small note on the "going down" carries its own irony, for Adonijah descends from David's city to seize what only David's word can give, and the height he has left is the height from which the true king will soon be enthroned. And Ellicott's note on the cry sharpens the offence: the acclamation that Israel reserved for the man God had chosen is being lavished on the man God had passed over, so that even the words of loyalty have been stolen and turned to the service of revolt.
26:
Nathan closes his recital as Bathsheba closed hers — on the list of the uninvited — and turns it into a personal appeal. The Reformed Gill notes whom he means: "But me, even me thy servant" is Nathan himself, "who was David's servant, his seer, and counsellor," and with him Zadok the priest, Benaiah, and "thy servant Solomon" — none of them called to Adonijah's feast. The emphatic repetition, "me, even me," presses the affront: the king's own prophet, the man through whom God had spoken about this very succession, was passed over as though he counted for nothing.

The witnesses read the omission exactly as they read it in Bathsheba's mouth — as evidence, not grievance. To leave Nathan uninvited was to shut out the one voice that carried heaven's designation of Solomon; to leave Zadok, Benaiah, and Solomon uninvited was to exclude the future high priest, the captain of the guard, and the appointed heir. The guest-list that Adonijah drew up to bind his party has become, in the mouths of both witnesses, the surest proof of his design, for a man who must exclude the prophet, the priest, the guard, and the heir has confessed by his very caution whose kingdom he was supplanting.

There is a further point in Nathan's naming of himself. Where Bathsheba spoke as a mother pleading for a son, Nathan speaks as a prophet whose exclusion is a slight upon his office and, through it, upon the word of God he bears. He had been "secretioribus consiliis," intimately acquainted with the mind of both God and the king, as Matthew Henry observes of the chapter; and so his being passed over is not merely a courtesy withheld but a signal that Adonijah's party meant to consult neither God nor David in the matter. The witnesses converge on a single reading: the very structure of Nathan's complaint, echoing Bathsheba's, doubles the testimony and drives home the same conclusion — that the exclusion of the LORD's prophet and the LORD's chosen heir marks the feast at En-rogel as a rebellion against the LORD's own settlement, and lays upon David the duty to answer it at once.
27:
Nathan ends with the sharpest form of his question: "Is this thing done by my lord the king, and thou hast not shewed it unto thy servant?" The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads it as the second of the two feigned questions that frame the prophet's speech, both "merely intended to draw out denial," yet both couched in the court's assumption that nothing of the kind could be done without the king's will. The form is deferential; the effect is to force David either to own the usurpation or, as he must, to disown it utterly.

The Reformed Gill lets Nathan's own words carry the weight — the prophet asks whether the thing was done "with his knowledge and consent, and by his orders." And the Puritan Matthew Henry unfolds the pointed logic Nathan presses upon the king. If David had indeed ordered it, then he had been faithful neither to God's word nor to his own, and had left his most trusted counsellor — the man employed to notify the mind of God concerning the succession — in the dark; "but, if my lord the king knows nothing of the matter, as certainly he does not, what daring insolence are Adonijah and his party guilty of!" Either horn of the question drives to the same end: David must act.

Henry names the purpose plainly: Nathan "endeavoured to incense David against them, that he might act the more vigorously for the support of Solomon's interest." The witnesses find in the verse a small model of faithful counsel. Nathan does not lecture the king or accuse him; he asks a question so framed that the king, in answering it, commits himself. And Henry draws the general lesson the scene affords: "good men would do their duty if they were reminded of it, and put upon it, and told what occasion there is for them to appear; and those who thus are their remembrancers do them a real kindness, as Nathan here did to David." The prophet's art is the art of the timely reminder — rousing a failing king not by reproach but by a question that turns his own conscience and his own authority toward the deed the hour requires. The interview has reached its crisis; the next verse will record the king's reply.
28:
David answers not with deliberation but with decision: "Call me Bathsheba." The Reformed Gill reads the summons as the fruit of the double witness. Observing "that Nathan confirmed the account that Bathsheba had given, and that it must be a matter of fact that Adonijah had usurped the throne," the king gives his orders — Bathsheba, who had withdrawn, is called back to receive his sworn answer.

The Wesleyan Clarke notes the courtesy of the arrangement: "she had gone out when Nathan came in, and he retired when she was re-admitted. Each had a separate audience." The two witnesses were kept apart, so that neither could be thought to have prompted the other, and the king's response, given to each in turn, rests on independent testimony. Clarke adds a shrewd observation about David's reserve — "to Nathan the king did not express any will," keeping his own counsel until he was ready to declare it in the most solemn form.

The witnesses read the verse as the turning-point of the whole interview. All the preceding scene — Bathsheba's plea, Nathan's timed entrance, the twice-told tale of the feast at En-rogel — has been aimed at rousing a sick and aged king to act, and here at last he stirs. The flash of the old David appears: the man who could get no heat, confined to his bed, suddenly issues a crisp command. That he calls for Bathsheba rather than answering Nathan is itself telling, for it is to Bathsheba, as the mother of the heir and the one to whom the oath was first sworn, that the confirming oath must be given. Gill and Clarke agree that the moment marks the recovery of the king's resolve; the languor that Nathan's boldness was meant to break has broken, and David, twice assured of the danger, moves at once to secure the succession he had promised. The next verses will record the oath itself; this brief command is the hinge on which the deliverance turns, the point at which counsel becomes action and the usurpation's undoing begins. And there is a fitness in the king's choosing to answer through Bathsheba rather than the prophet: the oath had first been sworn to her, and to her it must be renewed, so that the mother who had risked the plea receives the assurance of the son's crown from the king's own lips.
29:
David answers with an oath, and the witnesses hear in his form of swearing both the solemnity of the act and the gratitude of a life near its end. The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies the formula as "a characteristic adjuration of David," found also at 2 Samuel 4:9, "but now peculiarly appropriate in the old man, who was so near the haven of rest, after all the storms of life." To swear by "the Lord that hath redeemed my soul out of all distress" is, Ellicott notes, of a piece with the climax of David's great psalm, where he calls God "my strength and my Redeemer" (Psalms 19:14).

The Reformed Gill marks the escalation: "to his former oath, he added another for greater confirmation." David does not merely reaffirm; he binds himself afresh, so that the promise once sworn to Bathsheba is now sealed a second time in the presence of prophet and queen. And the Puritan Matthew Henry hears in the chosen words a dying saint's testimony. The oath "carries in it a grateful acknowledgment of the goodness of God to him, in bringing him safely through the many difficulties and hardships which had lain in his way," which David "now makes mention of to the glory of God" — as Jacob did on his deathbed, blessing "the God which fed me all my life long" and "the Angel which redeemed me from all evil" (Genesis 48:16).

The witnesses converge on the beauty of the moment. David seals the succession not with a bare legal formula but with a confession of grace: the God who had redeemed his soul out of every distress is called to witness the last great act of his reign. Henry adds that "dying saints ought to be witnesses for God, and speak of him as they have found," and that David perhaps swears thus "for the encouragement of his son and successor to trust in God in the distresses he also might meet with." So the oath does double duty — it secures Solomon's crown and it bequeaths to Solomon a testimony. The redeeming God who had carried David through the storms of a long and troubled life is the God into whose keeping he now commits both the kingdom and the king who will inherit it, and the very words of his swearing become a father's last sermon to his son.
30:
David repeats his oath in full: "Even as I sware unto thee by the Lord God of Israel, saying, Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me... even so will I certainly do this day." The Reformed Gill reads the verse as the king's deliberate ratification of all that had been alleged — David "owns and confirms the truth of what Nathan had suggested to Bathsheba, and she had asserted." The private oath, hitherto known only to the two, is now publicly avowed and made the ground of immediate action.

The Puritan Matthew Henry marks the two things the king does here. First, "he repeats his former promise and oath, owns that he had sworn unto her by the Lord God of Israel that Solomon would reign after him." Henry notes the tenderness of it in an aged man: "though he is old, and his memory begins to fail him, yet he remembers this," for "an oath is so sacred a thing that the obligations of it cannot be broken, and so solemn a thing that the impressions of it, one would think, cannot be forgotten." Second, David adds to the remembrance a fixed resolve — "even so will I certainly do this day, without dispute, without delay."

The witnesses find in the verse the meeting of two things that the whole interview has been driving toward: the sacredness of the oath and the urgency of the hour. Bathsheba had appealed to the oath; Nathan had roused the king to act on it; and now David, in a single sentence, binds the two together — the word once sworn will be performed today. Henry's emphasis on memory is apt, for the danger had been that a failing king might let the promise slip, whether from weakness or from the press of his own approaching death; but David's oath had graven itself too deep to be lost. And his "this day" answers Bathsheba's fear of the morrow: she had warned that once he slept with his fathers it would be too late, and he replies that it shall be done while he yet lives. The witnesses read the confirmation as the vindication of David's conscience — the same conscience Bathsheba had counted on — showing that for all his bodily decay the king's fidelity to a sworn word, and to the God in whose name he swore it, remained unbroken to the end.
31:
Bathsheba receives the king's oath with the deepest reverence and a blessing on his life. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe her leaving "with the deepest prostration and the utterance of a blessing, as an expression of her inmost gratitude," and explain the formula she uses: "May the king live for ever" was among the Israelites reserved "only... on occasions of special importance," though the Babylonians and Persians addressed their kings so as a matter of course (Daniel 2:4; Nehemiah 2:3).

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the grace of her response. She receives the assurance "with great complaisance to the king's person; she did reverence to him, while Adonijah and his party affronted him" — the contrast is pointed, the loyal wife bowing low while the rebel son shouted his own name at En-rogel. And she receives it "with hearty good wishes for the king's health: Let him live. So far was she from thinking that he lived too long that she prayed he might live for ever, if it were possible, to adorn the crown he wore and to be a blessing to his people." Henry turns it to a rule of unselfish love: "we should earnestly desire the prolonging of useful lives, however it may be the postponing of any advantages of our own."

The witnesses find in the moment a quiet rebuke to every grasping heart in the chapter. Adonijah had reckoned his father superannuated and good for nothing, and could not wait for his death; Bathsheba, whose son had most to gain from David's passing, prays that the old king may live for ever. Her blessing is the more striking for its timing, offered at the very instant her own dearest hope has been secured — she does not snatch at the succession she has just won, but blesses the man who granted it. Keil's note that the formula was kept for occasions of special weight underscores how solemn the moment felt to her: the succession settled, the peril lifted, the oath renewed, she answers not with triumph but with worship of her prince and prayer for his days. The contrast the chapter has been building — between those who love the king and those who covet his crown — is here drawn in a single sentence, and it falls wholly to Bathsheba's honour.
32:
David's languor breaks into swift command: "Call me Zadok the priest, and Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada." The grammatical-historical Ellicott seizes on the change of energy. "This sudden flash of the old energy in David, and the clear, terse directions which he gives," he writes, are "still more striking in contrast with the timidity and despondency with which, when far younger, he had received the news of Absalom's rebellion. For then he felt the coming of God's threatened chastisement; now he knows that this is passed, and that God is on his side." Keil and Delitzsch detail the orders: the three are to fetch "the servants of their lord" — the Crethi and Plethi of the bodyguard — and conduct Solomon, riding on the royal mule, down to Gihon to be anointed and proclaimed; for "when the king let any one ride upon the animal on which he generally rode himself, this was a sign that he was his successor," an honour with parallels among the Persians (Esther 6:8–9).

The Reformed Gill marks the pointed choice of Zadok, "not Abiathar the high priest, for he had joined Adonijah," but Zadok, "David's favourite priest, and for him the high priesthood was designed, as it was in a little time translated to him." The three summoned are exactly the loyal men Adonijah had passed over.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the whole scene into type. The unction Zadok and Nathan are to administer "was typical of the designation and qualification of the Messiah, or Christ, the anointed one, on whom the Spirit, that oil of gladness, was poured without measure" (Hebrews 1:9; Psalms 89:20); "and all Christians, being heirs of the kingdom" (James 2:5), "do from him receive the anointing" (1 John 2:27). The witnesses read the verse from three angles that do not conflict: to Ellicott it is the recovery of David's nerve now that the chastening for Bathsheba's sin has run its course; to Keil and Gill it is the machinery of a lawful succession, the royal mule and the loyal officers marking the true heir; to Henry it is a shadow of Christ, the Anointed, in whose unction his people share. David, roused at last, does everything in the open — mule, priest, prophet, and guard — that the usurper had counterfeited in secret.
33:
David's directions turn on a single royal token — his own mule — and the witnesses read the mount as a public proclamation of the heir. The Wesleyan Clarke states the custom bluntly: "No subject could use any thing that belonged to the prince, without forfeiting his life. As David offered Solomon to ride on his own mule, this was full evidence that he had appointed him his successor." Keil and Delitzsch say the same — to ride the king's own beast was the recognized sign of succession — and both note the escort, "the servants of your lord," the Crethi and Plethi of the bodyguard.

The Reformed Gill identifies those servants as David's own guards, the Cherethites and Pelethites, and adds a rabbinic inference drawn from the phrase "the servants of your lord": since David here calls himself the lord even of Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet, "the Jews from hence gather that a king is superior to an high priest."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott turns to the appointed place, Gihon, "breaking forth," clearly a spring in the valley under the walls of Jerusalem, with a watercourse later diverted by Hezekiah in preparing the city for siege (2 Chronicles 32:30). Whether it lay west, near the present Jaffa gate, or, as he thinks more probable, on the south — where the Targums read Siloam — he leaves in doubt, but he notes the southern site would agree with "the proximity to Adonijah's feast at En-rogel." The two courts are staged almost within earshot of each other. The witnesses converge on the meaning of the mule: everything about Solomon's installation is designed to be seen and known. Where Adonijah had gathered a private faction at a valley spring, David sends his son to another valley spring on the king's own mount, guarded by the king's own troops, so that the sign of true succession — the borrowed royal beast no subject might presume to ride — publicly answers the usurper's stolen acclamations. The place and the mule together announce that here is the king David has named.
34:
At the heart of the installation stands the anointing, and the witnesses read it as the act that makes the king. The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on its sacred character. This "solemn inauguration of royalty" is "marked emphatically as a religious consecration by the common phrase the Lord's anointed," then especially in use (compare 1 Samuel 16:6; 2 Samuel 1:14). The right to anoint, he judges, "was recognised as belonging to the prophetic order" (1 Kings 19:16), "inasmuch as it signified the outpouring of the Holy Spirit of the Lord" (compare Acts 10:38) — the same unction that had marked David's own private designation and public accession (1 Samuel 16:13; 2 Samuel 2:4).

The Reformed Gill notes that "it might be done by either" priest or prophet, "as the unctions of Saul and David show," and the Wesleyan Clarke adds the sequel: "after he has been anointed, make proclamation that he is king" — the trumpet publishing what the oil has accomplished.

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the point that anointing, not acclamation, is what God's king requires, and finds in it the mark that divides the true king from the usurper: "we do not find that Abiathar pretended to anoint Adonijah; he was made king by a feast, not by an unction. Whom God calls, he will qualify, which was signified by the anointing; usurpers had it not." Ellicott's own summary of the "completeness of the old King's provision" gathers the witnesses' agreement: "the riding on the King's mule, attended by the body-guard, marked the royal sanction; the anointing, the sanction of priest and prophet; and the acclamation the adhesion of the people." Solomon's kingship rests on a threefold seal — royal, sacred, and popular — where Adonijah's rested on a banquet. The witnesses do not dispute among themselves; they read one truth from several sides, that the oil poured at Gihon signifies the Spirit of the LORD coming upon his chosen, and that a crown so consecrated is of God, while a crown seized over meat and wine is of man's ambition only.
35:
David completes his orders with the enthronement: "ye shall come up after him... for he shall be king in my stead: and I have appointed him to be ruler over Israel and over Judah." The grammatical-historical commentators ask first why anointing was needed at all. Keil and Delitzsch explain that "both the anointing and the appointment of Solomon as prince over the whole of the covenant nation were necessary, because the succession to the throne had been rendered doubtful through Adonijah's attempt, and the aged king was still alive"; for, by "the unanimous opinion of the Rabbins," where the succession was undisputed "there was no anointing at all." The rite here answers a rival claim.

The witnesses then linger on the phrase "over Israel and over Judah." Charles Ellicott hears in it "the distinction, already tending to become a division," and traces its roots: David's accession over Israel had been sealed by "a league" with the elders (2 Samuel 5:3), where his accession over Judah had not, indicating from the first "a less absolute rule over the other tribes" — a looseness the revolts of Absalom and of Sheba had already exposed. Keil adds the sober forecast: the two are named together "because David had been the first to unite all the tribes under his sceptre, and after the death of Solomon Israel fell away from the house of David."

The Wesleyan Clarke sets out the ceremonial in orderly steps — the king-elect placed on the royal mule and brought to a public watercourse "denoting the perpetuity of the kingdom," anointed by priest and prophet, proclaimed by trumpet, and then "solemnly placed on the throne." The Reformed Gill pictures Solomon mounting the mule to "ride before them as their king, at the head of them, they following after, in token of their subjection." The witnesses read a scene of deliberate completeness shadowed by a distant grief: every act — anointing, proclamation, enthronement — binds the disputed crown fast to Solomon, yet the very words "Israel and Judah" carry within them the seed of the schism that will one day tear the kingdom in two. The installation could not be more secure for the son; the phrase could not be more ominous for the grandson.
36:
Benaiah answers for the loyal company with a single great word: "Amen." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold it: he "responded to the utterance of the royal will with the confirmatory Amen, thus saith Jehovah the God of my lord the king" — that is, "may the word of the king become a word of Jehovah his God, who fulfils what He promises" (Psalms 33:9) — and added the wish, "May Jehovah be with Solomon, as He was with David, and glorify his throne above the throne of David." Keil is careful to defend the prayer against the cynics: it was no mere "flattery of his paternal vanity," but a wish "for the prosperity of the monarchy," and one "fulfilled by God" (1 Kings 3:11).

The Reformed Gill notes that Benaiah spoke "in the name of the rest," so that his Amen carries the assent of all the loyal officers.

The Puritan Matthew Henry hears in the Amen "the language of his faith in that promise of God on which Solomon's government was founded," and draws from it a maxim of prayer: "if we say as God says in his word, we may hope that he will say as we say by his providence." Benaiah's word is at once submission and petition — he echoes the king's designation and asks God to seal it. Henry adds the generosity of the further prayer, that God would "make his throne greater than David's," and observes that David, far from resenting it, would heartily say Amen, for "the wisest and best man in the world desires his children may be wiser and better than he." The witnesses read the verse as the meeting of faith and providence: the king has spoken, and his faithful captain answers not with mere assent but with an Amen that reaches up to God, praying that the human designation may be ratified in heaven. And it was — the God who fulfils his promises did glorify Solomon's throne, so that Benaiah's prayer stands in the record as an instance of the very principle Henry names, the word of faith below answered by the word of providence above.
37:
Benaiah's prayer rises to its height: "As the Lord hath been with my lord the king, even so be he with Solomon, and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord king David." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, having defended the wish from the charge of flattery, read it as a genuine intercession for the kingdom's prosperity, and mark that God answered it. The Reformed Gill fills in its substance — that the Lord would "guide and direct him, protect and defend him, succeed and prosper him."

The Wesleyan Clarke pauses on the delicacy of the wish. "A wish of this kind a king will suffer in behalf of his son, but it is never in ordinary cases considered a compliment to say, I hope this child will make a better man than his father," for such words "seem to insinuate some reflections on his father's conduct or character." Yet from a father to a son the wish is welcome, and Clarke reaches for a classical echo, quoting Claudian's panegyric where a worn ruler commits the reins to his heir: "Behold, thy desire is accomplished.
