Even now thy son equals thee in worth; and what is still more desirable, surpasses thee."

The Puritan Matthew Henry names the grace that makes such a prayer possible: David "was not one of those that envy their children's greatness, and therefore he would not be disquieted at this prayer, nor take it as an affront, but would heartily say Amen to it." The witnesses converge on the beauty of an unenvying love. It is the mark of a great and godly heart to desire that those who come after should surpass those who went before — and Clarke's warning that such comparisons usually wound only sets in relief how free David was of the vanity that takes a son's advancement for a father's diminishment. There is a further reach in the prayer that the witnesses feel without labouring: Solomon's throne, made greater than David's, itself points beyond to the throne of great David's greater Son, whose kingdom exceeds his father's as far as heaven exceeds earth. Benaiah prays for a greater throne, and the God who was with David is with Solomon; the prayer is granted, and its ultimate granting lies in the King whose reign shall have no end.
38:
The orders are obeyed without a moment's delay, and the witnesses mark the promptness. Keil and Delitzsch note that "the anointing of Solomon was carried out immediately, as the king had commanded"; Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah, with the Cherethites and Pelethites, "went down, and caused Solomon to ride upon king David's mule, and brought him to Gihon." The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies the escort — the Cherethites and Pelethites, "the body-guard, perhaps of foreign troops, the executioners and runners, as some render them, to carry out the King's commands" (compare 2 Samuel 20:23). The Reformed Gill simply names "the three men that David sent for on this occasion," the loyal core of priest, prophet, and captain.

Keil adds a detail that will matter for the next verse: "the oil-horn out of the tent" was fetched not from the tabernacle at Gibeon but "from the tent set up by David for the ark of the covenant upon Mount Zion" (2 Samuel 6:17) — the nearer sanctuary, chosen for haste.

The witnesses read the verse as the swift translation of command into deed. Everything David had ordered in the preceding verses now happens at once, and the very speed is part of the point: the usurpation at En-rogel is still in progress, the feast not yet ended, and the true coronation must outrun it. The company that goes down to Gihon is exactly the company Adonijah had shut out — Zadok the priest who would supplant Abiathar, Nathan the prophet who bore God's designation, Benaiah the captain of the guard, and the foreign bodyguard sworn to David's person. Where Adonijah had gathered brothers and disgruntled officers to a private banquet, Solomon is escorted by the ordered instruments of the crown itself, riding the king's mule at the king's command. The witnesses see in the prompt obedience the collapse of the rival cause before it is even confronted: while the pretender still feasts, the appointed heir is already on his way to be anointed, and the kingdom is being settled in the very hour the usurper thinks it his. The narrator's economy of report — they went, they set him on the mule, they brought him down — mirrors the economy of the obedience: no debate, no delay, only the loyal execution of the king's will.
39:
The decisive act is done: "Zadok the priest took an horn of oil out of the tabernacle, and anointed Solomon." The witnesses agree on the sacred substance and divide only over its source. Charles Ellicott identifies it as "the sacred oil, the making of which is described in the law of the anointing (Exodus 30:22–30), reserved for anointing the tabernacle, its vessels, and the priests; and he resolves the question of where it was kept: "the Tabernacle proper was still at Gibeon" (2 Chronicles 1:3), "but a tent or tabernacle had been set up in Zion over the ark," and "the haste with which all was done would necessitate the taking the oil from the nearer source." Adam Clarke notes that horns were the common vessels for oil, and that this was "the holy anointing oil, which was laid up in the tabernacle, and which was used for the anointing of both priests and kings."

The Reformed Gill canvasses the harder textual question at length, weighing whether the oil came from the Mosaic tabernacle at Gibeon or the tent David had pitched for the ark on Zion, and reviewing Jarchi and Kimchi before leaving the point in learned suspense. The dispute is real but narrow — a matter of which sanctuary supplied the horn — and on the meaning of the act the witnesses are wholly one.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives that meaning its fullest voice. The horn of oil "denotes both power and plenty"; and the anointing itself is the sign that "whom God calls, he will qualify," the mark the usurper could never counterfeit — "usurpers had it not." Henry hears the type sounding through: "Christ signifies anointed, and he is the king whom God hath set upon his holy hill of Sion" (Psalms 2:6); "Christians also are made to our God, and by him, kings, and they have an unction from the Holy One" (1 John 2:20). So the witnesses converge: whatever tent yielded the horn, the oil that ran down Solomon's head was the holy oil of God's own appointing, signifying the Spirit that qualifies the king — a shadow of the Anointed One on whom that Spirit rests without measure, and of the anointing his people share.
40:
The anointing bursts into public jubilation: "all the people came up after him, and the people piped with pipes, and rejoiced with great joy, so that the earth rent with the sound of them." The grammatical-historical commentators relish the scene. Keil and Delitzsch picture the crowd accompanying Solomon "to the citadel of Zion, with flutes and loud acclamation, so that the earth nearly burst with their shouting," and note that the verb, "to burst in pieces" (compare 2 Chronicles 25:12), "is a hyperbolical expression for quaking." Adam Clarke reads the same figure through a common idiom — "the earth rent; we use a similar expression in precisely the same sense: they rent the air with their cries" — and takes "piped with pipes" to mean "they danced, sang, and played on what instruments of music they possessed."

Charles Ellicott draws the telling inference from the tumult: "the graphic description of the acclamation of the people indicates something more than conventional loyalty. The attempt of Adonijah relied on the support only of the great men, and perhaps the army, but had no popular following." The joy at Gihon is the verdict of the nation.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the meaning. The people's joy "declared their concurrence in the choice, and that he was not forced upon them, but cheerfully accepted"; for "the power of a prince can be little satisfaction to himself, unless he knows it to be a satisfaction to his people." And Henry lifts the shout into type: "every Israelite indeed rejoices in the exaltation of the Son of David," seeing here "as in a glass, Jesus, the Son of David and the Son of God, exalted to the throne of glory, notwithstanding all his enemies." The witnesses read one contrast across the verse. Adonijah had the great men and the swords, but the streets were silent for him; Solomon has the people, and the ground itself seems to quake with their gladness. A crown resting on a faction is a fragile thing, as the next verses will show; a crown greeted by a rejoicing nation stands firm. And in that popular joy for the anointed king the witnesses hear the far echo of a greater exaltation, when the true Son of David shall be owned with joy by all his people.
41:
The shout from Gihon reaches En-rogel just as Adonijah's banquet ends, and the witnesses relish the reversal. Keil and Delitzsch fix the moment: "the noise of this shouting reached the ears of Adonijah and his guests, when the feast was just drawing to a close," the music and acclamation carrying as far as the fountain of Rogel. Joab, the old soldier, is the first to catch the danger — "when Joab observed the sound of the trumpet, knowing what these tones must signify, he asked wherefore the sound of the city in an uproar." Charles Ellicott savours the touch: "it is one of the many life-like touches of the narrative that it is the old warrior Joab who, amidst the revelry of his companions, notices the sound of the trumpet, and the acclamation following," while "Adonijah affects to disregard it."

The Reformed Gill notes only that these were the guests Adonijah had "called," or invited — the party now about to be scattered.

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the scene a parable of false security. The tidings came "in the midst of their jollity: they had made an end of eating, and, it should seem, it was a great while before they made an end," so that "all the affair of Solomon's anointing was ordered and finished while they were at dinner, glutting themselves." Henry hears an apostolic echo: such men "serve not our Lord Christ, but oppose him," being those who "serve their own belly" and make a god of it. Their long feasting, he says, betrayed how "secure and confident of their interest" they were — like the old world and Sodom, "eating and drinking, secure and sensual, when their destruction came." The witnesses read one lesson across the verse. While the pretender's party lingered over their meat, the kingdom was quietly settled against them; the trumpet that should have been Adonijah's own now sounds his defeat. Joab's soldier's ear knows at once that an uproar in the city means a king proclaimed, and his alarm, set against Adonijah's studied unconcern, tells the reader that the feast at En-rogel is already over in every sense — the food eaten, the cause lost, the triumph fled.
42:
Into the faltering feast comes Jonathan, and the witnesses note both the man and the false hope Adonijah pins on him. Keil and Delitzsch record the greeting: "Come, for thou art a brave man and bringest good tidings," and read it as Adonijah "suppressing all anxiety with these words," for he "knew his father's will with regard to the succession, and the powerful and influential friends of Solomon." The bravado is a man talking down his own fear.

The Reformed Gill identifies the newcomer as Jonathan "the son of Abiathar the priest," whom "his father had left in the city, to observe what passed there, and give him notice of it" — a watcher posted by the conspirators, now arriving with news they had not foreseen. The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the man's pedigree from the story of Absalom's revolt, where Jonathan and Ahimaaz served as swift and trusted runners (2 Samuel 15:27; 2 Samuel 17:17–21); and he catches an irony — "it is curious that a similar greeting to his companion Ahimaaz is used by David" of old (2 Samuel 18:27), a saying then meant "as a kind of omen of good fortune," here bitterly misplaced.

The witnesses read the verse as the moment false confidence meets the truth. Adonijah greets the runner as a bearer of good news precisely because he is a "worthy man" — as though a good man could bring only welcome tidings to a bad cause. Henry, surveying the scene, turns the mistake into a maxim: "usurpers flatter themselves with the hopes of success, and those are commonly least timorous whose condition is most dangerous." The very grounds of Adonijah's cheer are the grounds of his ruin, for the honest messenger he welcomes has come to tell him that his father has already made Solomon king. There is a grim justice in it that the witnesses do not miss: the son of Abiathar, one of the two great defectors, is the one who must carry the death-blow of the conspiracy into its own banquet, so that the plot is undone from within its own ranks.
43:
Jonathan answers, and the single Hebrew particle with which he begins carries the whole weight of the reversal. Keil and Delitzsch render it "yea but," corresponding to the Latin imo vero, "an expression of assurance with a slight doubt" — Jonathan affirms, against Adonijah's hope, that the very opposite of good tidings has come. The Reformed Gill catches the same force: "nay, but it is not so as you imagine; it is not good tidings, but bad tidings to thee I bring." The runner turns Adonijah's welcome on its head.

The Puritan Matthew Henry frames the moment as the answer to the false confidence of the feast: "how can those who do evil deeds expect to have good tidings? No, the worthiest man will bring them the worst news, as the priest's son did here to Adonijah." Henry paraphrases the blow: "Verily, the best tidings I have to bring you is that Solomon is made king, so that your pretensions are all quashed."

The witnesses read the verse as the pivot on which the whole rebellion turns from triumph to terror. Jonathan does not soften it; he states at once the one fact that undoes everything — "our lord king David hath made Solomon king." The messenger whom Adonijah had greeted as a bringer of good news becomes the instrument of his overthrow, and the very worthiness on which Adonijah counted guarantees the honesty of the report. Henry's question lingers over the scene as its moral: a cause built on defiance of God and father cannot expect the providences of God to run in its favour. The good man's true word is bad news to the bad cause, and the feast that began with a stolen acclamation ends with a plain announcement that the true king is already enthroned. What Adonijah had hoped to hear confirmed — his own reign — he hears instead flatly reversed, and in a single sentence the banquet's confidence collapses. And there is a fittingness the witnesses feel in the messenger himself: the son of Abiathar, whose father was Solomon's rival's chief priestly backer, is made the mouth that announces Solomon's victory into the conspirators' own ears, so that the plot hears its own defeat from a voice within its ranks.
44:
Jonathan's report unrolls the whole of Solomon's installation, point by point, and the witnesses attend to its completeness. Keil and Delitzsch mark the rhetorical build: "the repetition of and also three times gives emphasis to the words, since every new point which is introduced with and also raises the thing higher and higher towards absolute certainty." Jonathan piles fact upon fact — the king sent Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah with the Cherethites and Pelethites; they set Solomon on the royal mule; they anointed him at Gihon; they are come up rejoicing; Solomon sits on the throne; the servants have blessed David; and David himself has worshipped — until no room for doubt or hope remains.

The Reformed Gill simply follows the narrative — "the king hath sent with him... to the fountain of Gihon" — letting Jonathan's orderly account carry its own force.

The witnesses read the verse as the deliberate demolition of Adonijah's cause by sheer accumulation of fact. Where the pretender's party had staged a private sanction — a feast, a priest, an acclamation among themselves — Jonathan reports a public one: the king's own command, the royal mule, the sacred oil, the anointing by prophet and priest, the rejoicing of the whole city, and the enthronement in the palace. Each detail answers and cancels a corresponding pretension at En-rogel. Adonijah had ridden nothing, been anointed by no one, and shouted his own name among his own guests; Solomon has been carried on the king's mule, anointed with the holy oil, and hailed by the nation. Keil's note on the mounting "and also" captures the effect: the messenger is not merely informing but overwhelming, building an unanswerable case that the true king reigns. By the time Jonathan reaches David's own worship in the closing verses of his speech, the conspirators have heard everything they most feared, told with a plainness that leaves no purchase for resistance. The feast is not merely interrupted; it is refuted. Gill's plain following of the sending only reinforces the effect, letting the sober catalogue of royal acts speak for itself against the vanished pomp of En-rogel.
45:
Jonathan reaches the heart of his report — the anointing itself — and the witnesses note how the two priests are joined in the act. Keil and Delitzsch record that "Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king in Gihon: and they are come up from thence rejoicing, so that the city rang again. This is the noise that ye have heard." The runner names the very sound that had first alarmed Joab, and traces it to its cause. The Reformed Gill attends to the shared action: "here the act of anointing is ascribed to them both," and he reconciles it — "Zadok very probably applied the oil to him, and Nathan might be some way or other assisting," for Nathan "was here present, not only as approving of it, but declaring it as a prophet, that it was according to the will of God, as well as of the king."

The witnesses read the verse as the answer to the mystery of the noise. The uproar that had broken into the feast, that Joab's ear had recognized and Adonijah had affected to ignore, is now explained: it was the city ringing with the joy of Solomon's anointing. Gill's care to give each of the two their part is more than harmonizing detail; it underscores that both the priestly and the prophetic sanction rested on Solomon, the oil applied by the priest and the divine will declared by the prophet. This is precisely the double authority Adonijah's coronation lacked. At En-rogel there had been a priest, Abiathar, but no prophet, and no word from God; here priest and prophet act together, and the anointing is at once a sacred rite and a declaration of the LORD's will. Jonathan's phrase "this is the noise that ye have heard" lands with quiet finality, tying the sound in their ears to the fact of Solomon's kingship. The feasters had heard the city rejoice without knowing why; now they know, and the knowing is the end of their cause. So the very sound that had troubled the feast is turned into the herald of Solomon's reign, and the two who anointed him — priest and prophet together — stand as the double warrant the pretender's coronation had never possessed.
46:
Jonathan adds the decisive fact: "Solomon sitteth on the throne of the kingdom." The Reformed Gill reads it as the seal of all the rest — Solomon "was placed to exercise his regal power when returned to Jerusalem, as a further token and confirmation of his being really and actually king." The anointing at Gihon has been followed by the enthronement in the city; the office is not merely conferred but occupied.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the moment in its narrative order: "Jonathan's announcement here takes up the narrative of events after" the rejoicing at Gihon. "The public enthronement in the palace" — the very thing David had ordered when he said Solomon "shall come and sit upon my throne" — "follows the anointing and acceptance by the acclamations of the people, as an integral part of the inauguration of royalty." The three sanctions David had provided are now complete: royal mule, sacred oil, popular acclaim, and at last the throne itself.

The witnesses read the verse as the point past which Adonijah's cause cannot recover. To be anointed was to be designated; to be enthroned is to be installed. Solomon is no longer merely the king David named or the man the people hailed, but the king who sits and reigns, exercising the royal power in the royal seat. Ellicott's insistence that the enthronement is "an integral part" of the inauguration matters here, for it marks the completion of a lawful process against which the feast at En-rogel had nothing to set but noise and appetite. The throne that Adonijah had reached for while his father yet lived is now filled by the son his father chose, and filled with David's own knowledge and blessing. When the messenger reports that Solomon sits on the throne of the kingdom, he reports the one fact that turns a proclamation into a reign, and every man at the failing feast understands that the crown they had gathered to seize is not merely promised elsewhere but already worn. The witnesses let the fact stand in its bare finality: not the promise of a throne, nor the noise of a crowd, but a king seated and reigning, with his father's knowledge and the nation's joy behind him.
47:
Jonathan's report rises to its most affecting scene — the king's servants blessing David, and the old king bowing in worship on his bed. The Reformed Gill explains the courtiers' errand: they came "to give him thanks for the wise and good provision he had made before his death for the welfare of the kingdom, by making Solomon his son king in his stead," which "showed that they highly approved of it, and were ready to swear allegiance to Solomon" — so that "Adonijah had nothing to hope for from them." Their blessing was, "God make the name of Solomon better than thy name, and make his throne greater than thy throne."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the king's response: "the king bowed himself, that is, in worship" (compare Genesis 47:31), "at once joining in the prayer of his servants, and thanking God for the fulfilment of His promise." The Wesleyan Clarke adds a realist's touch about the source of the news — David "was at this time confined to his own house, and probably to his bed, and could not possibly see these ceremonies; therefore his confidential servants came and told him," and he wonders how Jonathan had gathered so much so quickly.

The witnesses read the verse for the picture at its centre: a dying king who, hearing that his son is safely enthroned, turns the good news into an act of worship. The servants' prayer that Solomon's name and throne might exceed David's own is exactly the wish Benaiah had uttered at Gihon, and David, far from resenting it, bows and blesses God. Matthew Henry, surveying the whole report, draws the note of a good man's deathbed contentment — "it is a great satisfaction to good men, when they are going out of the world, to see the affairs of their families in a good posture, their children rising up in their stead to serve God and their generation." The last strength of David is spent not in triumph over a defeated rival but in adoration of the God who has kept his word, and Jonathan's account of that bow on the bed is, to the feasters, the final proof that the whole nation and the king himself stand with Solomon.
48:
Jonathan closes his report with David's own doxology: "Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it." The Reformed Gill notes only that the king was now "in a proper posture for an address to God" — bowed upon his bed in worship, as the previous verse described — before pouring out this thanksgiving.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the words their full weight as the utterance of a dying saint. He hears David bless "the Lord God of Israel, who, as Israel's God, for Israel's good, has brought this matter to such a happy issue, my eyes even seeing it." Henry fastens on that last clause: it is "a great satisfaction to good men, when they are going out of the world, to see the affairs of their families in a good posture, their children rising up in their stead to serve God and their generation, and especially to see peace upon Israel and the establishment of it." David asked no more than to see the succession secured before his eyes closed, and God granted him even that.

The witnesses read the verse as the spiritual summit of the chapter. All the intrigue and haste — Nathan's counsel, Bathsheba's plea, the ride to Gihon, the anointing and enthronement — resolve at last into a bedridden old man's blessing of God. David does not congratulate himself on outwitting Adonijah, nor exult over the scattering of the rebels; he blesses the LORD who "hath given one to sit on my throne this day." The kingship is received as a gift, and the seeing of it with his own eyes as a mercy beyond his deserving. There is a quiet fulfilment here of the promise that David's seed should sit upon his throne, and Henry's phrase "for Israel's good" catches the largeness of the king's joy — it is not for his house only but for the nation's peace that he gives thanks. The very report meant to crush Adonijah's hope thus ends, in Jonathan's mouth, with the sound of David worshipping, so that the last word the feasters hear is not a threat but a benediction, and it seals their cause as hopeless against a king whom God himself has enthroned.
49:
With Jonathan's report finished, the feast dissolves in fear: "all the guests that were with Adonijah were afraid, and rose up, and went every man his way." The Reformed Gill marvels at the collapse: though "many of them were military men, the general of the army, and the captains thereof," yet "they were struck with a panic, their courage failed them, they had no spirit left in them, their hearts became as weak as water." Had they exerted themselves, "betaken themselves to arms," they might have given Solomon much trouble; but instead they scattered, and Gill reads the hand of God in it — "no doubt this panic was of God."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the same wonder into a historical observation: "nothing is more striking than the sudden and humiliating collapse of the attempt of Adonijah, strongly supported as it was by Joab and Abiathar, in contrast with the formidable character of the rebellion of Absalom." He offers his recurring explanation — that "the royal power had been greatly consolidated" in David's last peaceful years, and that "the usurpation of Adonijah, not being viewed as a rebellion against David, but only a presumption on his favour, was accordingly crushed at once by the expression of his will." And he marks the telling detail that "of all the conspirators Adonijah alone seems to have feared punishment at this time," his accomplices being "apparently allowed to disperse in safety."

The witnesses read the rout as the vindication of the whole chapter's theme. A conspiracy that had gathered generals and a high priest melts away at the mere news that David has spoken, without a blow struck. Gill sees the finger of God unnerving the mighty; Ellicott sees a settled throne and a rebellion that was never quite rebellion, collapsing the instant the king's real will is known. The two readings meet in a single truth: the cause that ran against God's designation and David's oath had no strength to stand once that designation was declared. Matthew Henry's earlier word fits the scene exactly — "combinations formed upon evil principles will soon be dissolved, when self-interest calls another way." The men who had shouted "God save king Adonijah" now flee each to his own house, and the feast that began as a coronation ends as a stampede.
50:
Adonijah alone does not flee homeward but to the altar: "he feared because of Solomon, and arose, and went, and caught hold on the horns of the altar." The Wesleyan Clarke reads his mind: knowing "he had usurped the kingdom" without his father's consent, and seeing that Solomon was now appointed and would be respected, "he knows well that his case is hopeless; he therefore took sanctuary, and, fleeing to the tabernacle, laid hold on one of the horns of the altar, as if appealing to the protection of God against the violence of men," for "the altar was a privileged place, and it was deemed sacrilege to molest a man who had taken refuge there."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains the gesture and its limits. The horns were "projections from the altar," to which the victims were bound and on which the blood was sprinkled (Exodus 29:12); "to take hold of them was, of course, to claim the right of sanctuary" — a right, however, "which the Law, ruled as usual by moral considerations, formally denied to wilful murder" (Exodus 21:14), and which would accordingly be refused hereafter to Joab (1 Kings 2:28–31). By his very act, Ellicott suggests, Adonijah "seems to represent his usurpation as one of those acts of haste and inadvertency, to which alone sanctuary was conceded."

The Reformed Gill states the fear plainly: Adonijah dreaded "lest he should seize him as an usurper and traitor, and put him to death." The witnesses read the flight to the altar as the confession of a beaten man. Matthew Henry draws out the irony that runs through it: "he had despised Solomon as not worthy to be his guest, but now he dreads him as his judge." The man who that morning would not seat his brother at his feast now clings to the altar for his life, throwing himself upon the mercy of the very king he had sought to supplant — and, through the altar, upon the mercy of God signified in the sacrifices offered there. Henry turns it to a warning: many who neglect the great salvation in their day of security will, "under the arrests of the terrors of the Lord," gladly catch hold of the horns of the altar when it is too late. The horns that crown the place of atonement become the last hope of the usurper, and his grip on them is the measure of his fall.
51:
Word of Adonijah's flight comes to the new king: "Behold, Adonijah feareth king Solomon: for, lo, he hath caught hold on the horns of the altar, saying, Let king Solomon swear unto me to day that he will not slay his servant with the sword." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the change of title that betrays the change of fortune — the report has Adonijah owning "king Solomon" and calling himself "his servant," the very submission his morning's revolt had denied. The Reformed Gill adds only that it was "some of his courtiers" who brought Solomon the news.

The witnesses read the verse as the moment the usurper's pride turns to petition. He who had said "I will be king" now asks that the king he defied would swear not to slay him; he who had feasted "all the king's sons" save Solomon now begs Solomon for his life. The demand for an oath is itself a token of his desperation and of his lingering distrust: he will not leave the altar on a bare word but requires the same solemn form — a swearing "this day" — by which David had secured Solomon's own crown. There is a grim symmetry the witnesses feel without labouring it. The chapter had turned on David's oath, sworn in the name of the LORD, that Solomon should reign; now the defeated rival, clinging to the horns of the altar, asks an oath in turn that he may live. Matthew Henry marks the greatness of the reversal in a single stroke: "he that in the morning was grasping at a crown is before night begging for his life." The altar that Adonijah's own sacrificial feast had aped at En-rogel is now his only refuge, and the king whose anointing he had tried to forestall holds his life in his hand. Everything now waits on Solomon's answer, and the manner of that answer will set the tone of the reign just begun. Gill's brief notice that courtiers carried the word only heightens the contrast: the man who had gathered his own court at En-rogel now depends on Solomon's court to plead for his life, and waits, clinging to the altar, on the mercy of the king he wronged.
52:
Solomon answers with a mercy carefully hedged: "If he will shew himself a worthy man, there shall not an hair of him fall to the earth: but if wickedness shall be found in him, he shall die." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the condition exactly — "if he shall be brave," a worthy man, "not a hair of his head shall be injured" (compare 1 Samuel 14:45); "but if evil be found in him," if "he render himself guilty of a fresh crime, he shall die." The Wesleyan Clarke gives the same terms in plain speech: "if, from henceforth, he behave well, show himself to be contented, and not endeavor to make partisans, or stir up insurrections among the people, he shall be safe; but if he act at all contrary to this, he shall die." The Reformed Gill adds that to "shew himself a worthy man" is to be careful "not to offend for the future, particularly the crimes of sedition, rebellion, and treason."

The witnesses read Solomon's word as wisdom tempering clemency with justice. He grants Adonijah his life, but on condition, and the condition is precisely the good behaviour the rebel had just failed to show. Matthew Henry weighs the young king's judgment: he "considered that Adonijah was his brother, and that it was the first offence," and hoped that one "so soon made sensible of his error" might yet prove "a peaceable, and even a serviceable subject." Yet the pardon is conditional, for if Adonijah remain "turbulent and aspiring, this offence shall be remembered against him." Henry lifts the mercy into type: "thus the Son of David receives those to mercy that have been rebellious; if they will return to their allegiance, and be faithful to their Sovereign, their former crimes shall not be mentioned against them; but if still they continue in the interests of the world and the flesh, this will be their ruin." The witnesses find in the sparing of Adonijah a foreshadowing of a greater King's dealing with pardoned rebels — clemency freely offered, yet holding the peace of the kingdom, and answered either by loyalty or by a final judgment.
53:
The chapter closes with the pardon enacted: "So king Solomon sent, and they brought him down from the altar. And he came and bowed himself to king Solomon: and Solomon said unto him, Go to thine house." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the scene for its restraint. Solomon "had him fetched down from the altar," and when Adonijah "fell down before the king," doing homage to him as king, "he gave him his life and freedom in the words, Go to thy house." The dismissal, Keil insists, "does not imply his banishment from the court"; rather, "Solomon did not wish to commence his own ascent of the throne by infliction of punishment, and therefore presented the usurper with his life on the condition that he kept himself quiet." The Reformed Gill notes only the altar's height — Adonijah is "brought down," the place being "built upon an hill."

The Wesleyan Clarke hears in "Go to thine house" a mild but real restriction: it intimated "that he should have no place about the king's person, nor under the government," so that "Adonijah must have seen that he stood continually on his good behaviour." (Clarke then appends his long extract from Friar Bacon's cure of old age, returning at the last to the medical curiosity with which he began the chapter.)

The witnesses read the ending as a portrait of a reign begun in mercy and stability. Adonijah, who had grasped at the crown, now bows before it and is sent home alive; Solomon, who might have opened his reign with blood, opens it with a pardon. Matthew Henry gathers the whole into its evangelical shape: Solomon "not only gave him his life, but his estate, thus establishing his throne by mercy," and the chapter that began with a dying David and a scheming son ends with the Son of David enthroned and clement, receiving even a rebel to grace. Yet the mercy is watchful, not indulgent — "Go to thy house" is at once a reprieve and a warning — and the reader who knows the next chapter will feel the condition hanging over the peace. The witnesses close where the chapter closes: the true king reigns, the rebel is spared on terms, and a throne established by mercy stands ready to be defended by justice.
